mappings.rationaleAI: Provides comprehensive, structured coverage of the entire history-of-medicine outline, from prehistoric and ancient Eastern and Western practice (A.1) through 19th-century scientific medicine including bacteriology and germ theory (A.2), 20th-century chemotherapy, immunology, and endocrinology (A.3), and the emergence of 20th-century surgical specialties (A.4).

Pasteur's verification of germ theory and identification of disease-producing organisms directly address A.2.c (establishment of bacteriology); his creation of vaccines for anthrax, chicken cholera, and rabies maps precisely to A.3.b (advances in immunology and bacterial/viral vaccine development).

Freud founded psychoanalysis and transformed clinical psychiatry, making him directly relevant to B.1.g (psychiatry as a hospital residency specialty); his influence on 20th-century medicine and his clinical work with neurosis also connect to A.3 (medicine in the 20th century).

Hippocrates is the central figure in the beginning of systematic medicine in the Greco-Roman world (A.1.d), and his contributions — clinical observation, humoral theory, disease categorization, and the Hippocratic Oath — map exactly to A.1.d.ii (Hippocrates' theories and the charter of medical conduct).

As the broad overview article for the entire discipline, it covers the history of medicine and surgery (A), clinical and nonclinical medical specialties (B), and affiliated disciplines including nursing and dentistry (C), making it relevant to all three top-level outline divisions.

The hospital article addresses the genesis of the hospital and its role in medical education (A.1.h.i), and its typology of general, specialized, and teaching hospitals maps directly to the hospital residency specialties in B.1, including radiology, surgery, and other clinical departments.

The Islamic Golden Age includes substantial treatment of medieval Islamic medicine and surgery, corresponding to A.1.e.i's coverage of Arabian medicine as a reservoir of medical learning that preserved and advanced Greco-Roman medical heritage through the Middle Ages.

Dentistry is the exact subject of outline path C.1 (history and practice of dentistry), covering its historical development as the earliest specialized medical discipline, its subspecialties, and its connections to broader medical practice including radiology and systemic disease.

Physiology addresses further advances in physiology relevant to A.2.b in the 19th-century rise of scientific medicine, and maps to B.3 as one of the nonclinical specialties and basic medical sciences (medical physiology and pathological physiology) explicitly named in the outline.

The disease article covers etiology, pathogenesis, prevention, and treatment across infectious, deficiency, hereditary, and physiological categories, connecting to 19th-century germ theory and pathogenesis (A.2), 20th-century advances in treatment (A.3), and public health as a clinical specialty (B.2.d).

Avicenna's Canon of Medicine was a standard medical text at European universities through the 17th century, directly addressing A.1.e.i (contributions of Arabian medicine as a reservoir of learning); his synthesis of Greek and Islamic medical traditions also connects to A.1.c (systematized medicine in the Orient and its transmissions).

The Scientific Revolution covers Vesalius's anatomical improvements relevant to A.1.f (improvements in anatomical theory and surgery in the Renaissance), and the experimental method and discoveries concerning blood circulation that map directly to A.1.g (medicine in the 17th century).

HIV/AIDS engages immunology and the challenge of viral vaccine development (A.3.b.ii), while also being a core subject within immunology as a clinical specialty (B.2.f), including antiretroviral therapy and its intersection with public health and tropical medicine.

Surgery covers the entire 20th-century surgery outline (A.4) including the emergence of surgical specialties, treatment of wounds, blood transfusions, and inhalation anesthesia, and is also the specific hospital residency specialty named in B.1.b.

The vaccine article directly addresses advances in immunology (A.3.b), covering both improvements in bacterial vaccines such as typhoid, diphtheria, and tuberculosis (A.3.b.i) and the development of viral vaccines for yellow fever, influenza, polio, and measles (A.3.b.ii).

The Middle Ages article covers the institutional role of monasteries in preserving Greco-Roman medical knowledge and the contributions of Arabian medicine — the two themes explicitly identified in A.1.e (medicine from the fall of Rome through the Middle Ages).

The Renaissance article addresses the anatomical and surgical improvements of the period alongside the work of figures like Paracelsus, corresponding to A.1.f (medicine in the Renaissance, including improvements in anatomical theory and surgery).

Ancient Greece provides the cultural and intellectual context for the beginning of systematic medicine in the Greco-Roman world (A.1.d), covering the philosophical traditions and institutions from which Hippocratic medicine emerged.

Ancient Rome covers the spread of Greek medical teachings to Rome and Galen's acceptance as the dominant medical authority, corresponding precisely to A.1.d.iii (the spread of Greek teachings to Rome and the acceptance of Galen).

The Enlightenment article addresses 18th-century institutional and intellectual developments directly connected to A.1.h, including the genesis of organized medical schools, hospitals, and the beginnings of medical specialization through rational reform.

Florence Nightingale is the founder of modern nursing, making her directly relevant to C.3 (history and practice of nursing), a coverage-priority path; her statistical innovations and hospital reform work also connect to 19th-century advances in medicine (A.2).

Curie founded radioisotope cancer treatment and deployed mobile X-ray units, directly addressing medical technology and biomedical instrumentation (A.3.d.iv) and cancer research through novel therapeutic application (A.3.d.ii).
